name: Chicken Teriyaki
description: Just One Cookbook's classic Japanese Chicken Teriyaki — pan-seared chicken thighs glazed in a simple 4-ingredient homemade teriyaki sauce that cooks right in the pan and caramelizes to a shiny glaze. No bottled sauce, no cornstarch. Serve over steamed rice.
recipeIngredient: Steamed Japanese short-grain rice, to serve
recipeInstructions: Combine the sauce ingredients (soy sauce, mirin, sake, sugar) in a microwave-safe bowl; microwave until hot and the sugar dissolves. Set aside.
Place the thighs smooth side down. Score the meat every 1/2 inch without cutting through (deeper slits on the thick parts), pressing to flatten to an even thickness after each cut.
Season the chicken lightly with salt and pepper.
Heat a large frying pan over medium-high. Add the oil; when hot, lay the chicken smooth side down. Cook 5 minutes, pressing firmly with a spatula for a good sear.
Flip, press again 5–10 seconds, then reduce to low, cover, and steam 3 minutes.
Remove the lid; wipe excess grease and protein from the pan with a paper towel.
Raise heat to medium and add the teriyaki sauce. Tilt the pan and spoon the sauce over the chicken repeatedly until well coated and cooked through; transfer the chicken to a board.
Keep cooking the sauce until thickened to a glaze; pour into a bowl.
Slice the chicken into bite-size pieces (sear with a torch or under the broiler if you like). Serve over steamed rice, drizzled with the sauce.
author: Namiko Chen / Just One Cookbook

ai.cliffNotes: Pan-seared chicken thighs glazed in a homemade soy-mirin-sake-sugar teriyaki that reduces in the pan to a shiny glaze — under 20 minutes, no bottled sauce. Serve over rice with a simple green.
keyTips: Score and flatten the thighs to an even thickness — uneven thighs cook unevenly and dry out at the thin edges; flattening gives you juicy, evenly-cooked meat.
Use skin-on thighs if you can: the skin acts as a protective layer that keeps the meat juicy, and the rendered fat helps the glaze cling.
Pat the chicken dry and sear skin/smooth side down first, pressing with a spatula — dry skin sears crisper and faster.
After searing, cover and steam 3 minutes to cook through gently without overcooking, then wipe the excess grease so the sauce glazes instead of greasing.
No cornstarch — let the sauce reduce naturally; the sugar caramelizes into the glossy teriyaki shine. Spoon it over the chicken repeatedly (an extra step, but it's what builds the lacquered finish).
Balance the richness at the table with steamed rice plus a simple green (blanched green beans) or a cucumber salad.
ai.source: Claude (prose-mined from the Just One Cookbook article)
